data.title: International Annual Returns, The Breakout In The Nikkei, And Top 10 Holdings
data.description:
It’s no mystery that international has outperformed the S&P 500 in 2025, and not by a small amount.

The dollar’s direction will likely determine whether 2026 is a repeat of 2025 for the asset class, and the top strategists on Wall Street, like David Kelly of JPMorgan and Rick Rieder of BlackRock, have both said the dollar is still too strong and needs to come down more.

Japan and the Nikkei

Callum Thomas of TopDownCharts, who hails from New Zealand, has been posting on X and LinkedIn for years and does a good job.

This chart above from TopDown Charts shows the breakout in the Nikkei 225, the Nikkei’s first poke of its head above the 1989 high - and it happened in just the last year.

This blog is playing the breakout via the EWJ or the iShares MSCI Japan Index Fund ETF.

Here are the annual returns for EWJ as of 10/31/25:

  • 1-year: +25.52%
  • 3-year: +20.79%
  • 5-year: +9.35%
  • 10-year: +7.25%
  • 15-year: +6.77%

Morningstar’s returns track something called “earliest available” annual return, which for the EWJ would be its first trading month, which is December 1991, or two years after the Nikkei’s peak in 1989, and that “earliest available annual return” is just +2.85% over the last 33 years.

The 5-year return is still less than 10%, and note the spreadsheet at the top of this page, and how the 10 and 15-year returns are still below 10%. In the last 12 to 18 months, international as an asset class has closed the gap with the S&P 500, but the sentiment around non-US is far less frothy than the US.

Top 10–12 Holdings as of 10/31/25:

  • JP Morgan Income Fund (JMSIX): YTD return +6.33%
  • JPMorgan (JPM) common equity: YTD return +32.11%
  • JPMorgan Developed Int’l Value (JFEAX): YTD return +36.05%
  • Amazon common stock (AMZN): +11.32%
  • Microsoft (MSFT) common equity: +23.44%
  • Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) equity ETF: +23.51%
  • Schwab (SCHW) common equity: +28.81%
  • Alphabet (GOOGL) common equity: +48.87%
  • IBM common equity (IBM): +42.13%
  • Tesla common equity (TSLA): +13.05%
  • iShares Emerging Market ex-China (EMXC): +31.81%
  • iShares +20-year Treasuries (TLT): +6.80%

S&P 500 YTD return as of 10/31/25 was +17.40%

  • This blog manages $55 million via separate accounts at Charles Schwab and has done so since 1995. There are 4 to 5 larger accounts that represent roughly 20% of the $55 million, and several of those are 100% equity.
  • The three JPMorgan positions were not intentional, although JPMorgan has been very supportive of this blog’s work, and when this blog was looking for an international fund/ETF in late ’24 to reallocate to, the JFEAX fund – even in ’24 – was performing ahead of those funds followed.
  • Most client accounts are managed on a “balanced” basis with allocations targeted towards 60% equity/40% fixed income mix.
  • The large-cap tech positions have been trimmed over the last 12 to 14 months, since this secular bull market is now in its 15th year, with the start coming on March 9, 2009. Rather than rebalance at the end of a quarter, the rebalancing is done almost continually, with rotation from what has outperformed materially to an asset class, sector, or stock that has underperformed materially. It takes time to sell larger positions, often due to tax constraints, and it often takes time to build out-of-favor positions into material positions.
  • The three newer positions in 2025 were JFEAX, EMXC, and IBM. Not on the top 10 list yet, but shares are still being added are Boeing (BA), Japan Nikkei ETF (EWJ) and Nike (NKE).

Summary/conclusion

Having lived through the 2000s decade, when international investing completely missed the late 1990s bull market and only began to outperform in mid-2000 through 2007, only to collapse once again in 2008, 2025 was likely one of the best years of non-US asset class returns since the late 2000s.

In my opinion (and it’s only that), the “China-centric” gravitational pull from 2000 to 2007, when China was growing 15% per year, from 2000 to 2010, 2011 (and maybe longer), may be the reason the international outperformance lasted only several years in the 2000s. Everything non-US was dependent on that Chinese economic engine.

The fact that China is no longer as dominant as it once was may actually be a reason international returns could see a longer streak of high single-digit, low double-digit returns over the next few years, given the asset class has been dead money for so long.

None of this is advice or a recommendation but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. None of this information may be updated, and if updated, may not be done in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: FOF: Monthly Income With Solid Total Return
data.description:
I rate Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund (NYSE:FOF) a Buy, for income-focused investors who are looking for long term buy and hold investments. The fund is focused on total return, generating monthly income while building capital appreciation. FOF is a fund of funds holding a highly diverse set of investments, including bonds from PIMCO, commodities such as gold and silver with Sprott, and various BlackRock Capital allocation funds.

The consistent monthly distribution is a reliable source of income for retirement or to use for dollar cost averaging. Given the current environment, FOF has the potential to act as a defensive income fund, weathering a potential correction. Although its trading at a slight premium to NAV (1.86%), the distribution of 7.96% makes the fund appealing in my view.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the fund are:

  • Pays a consistent monthly distribution of $0.087 since 2016.
  • Currently yields 7.96% annually, goes ex-dividend on 12/9/25.
  • Highly diversified fund composed of top tier closed-end funds.
  • Actively managed by Cohen & Steers, a highly reputable money manager.
  • Fund has been running for 19 years.
  • Low expense ratio of 0.95%.
  • Strong total return over 5 years (79.74%) and 10 years (183.4%).

Full disclosure, I own shares in FOF, and a few other Cohen & Steers closed end funds, which have performed well over time. In fact, I recently published an analysis on all of the C&S closed-end funds, recommending certain picks in the article: “Best High Yield Income Funds from Cohen & Steers.” Click on article to read.

Seeking Alpha Quant does not cover the fund.

Cohen & Steers – Leading Global Investment Managers

Cohen & Steers, Inc. (CNS) is based in New York City and has been in business for over thirty-five years with $90.9 billion in assets under management (AUM). 74% of the AUM is focused on listed real assets, 19% on alternative income and the remaining 7% on multi-strategy funds. With six offices globally, C&S’s senior management have twenty years on average of investment experience. The company offers both private and public funds in the form of open and closed-end funds.

C&S serves a primarily institutional client. They offer collective investment trusts (CITs) for qualified defined benefit and defined contribution plans. C&S also provide customized investment strategies to institutional investors, such as large pension funds, endowments, and foundations.

Founded in 1986, Marty Cohen and Bob Steers launched the first public mutual fund specializing in real estate securities. In 1988, the company launched the first closed-end real estate securities fund. Per their Factsheet:

Cohen & Steers is a leading global investment manager specializing in real assets and alternative income, focused on delivering attractive returns, income and diversification.

Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund

According to the Fact Sheet:

“The investment objective of the Fund is to seek to achieve total return consisting of high current income and potential capital appreciation through investments in the common stock of closed-end management investment companies that invest significantly in equity or income-producing securities.”

The top 10 holdings account for 45.9% of the portfolio as listed below. Notice the sectors of each fund including commodities, equity, and fixed income. Also the top 7 funds are trading at a discount to NAV.

The fund is diversified, as seen in the chart below, into equity, commodity, fixed income, and municipal funds, making it ideal for buy and hold, in my view. The equity component which comprises 54% of the fund will generate capital appreciation for total return, and potentially capital gains for distribution.

Performance To Date

Over the past 10 years, an investment of $10,000 in FOF would have grown to $28,340 with dividends reinvested. If an investor took the distributions for income, the fund would still be worth $11,580. This means for most investors, the share price grew while they collected distributions. This feature is especially attractive for retired investors relying on a monthly income or institutional investors constructing defined benefit plans, such as pensions.

In comparison to benchmarks over the past 10 years, FOF produced a total return of 183.4%, outperforming the real estate sector represented by (VNQ) but falls short of the S&P 500 (SPY), as seen in the chart below.

Valuation

In 2016, the fund switched from quarterly to monthly distributions, paying a consistent $0.0870 per share every month.

Competition

In a comparison to peers, FOF has the lowest expense ratio, at just less than 1%. The turnover of funds at 30% is typical for the sector, but still shows the fund is actively managed. Beyond the 8.0% distribution yield, FOF generated a 14.0% 5-year price performance.

Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF (PCEF) is the largest fund ranked by assets under management (AUM). The dividend yield is similar at 8.0% but the share price has declined -6.5%.

RiverNorth Opportunities (RIV) pays the highest distribution rate of 13.2% but that comes with a declining share price, -20.3% over the past 5 years. It also has a high turnover rate at 58%. Since the last time I wrote about this fund, RIV increased its AUM to pass CEFS.

Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS) is one of the most popular funds discussed on Seeking Alpha, run by activist investor Saba Management. Typically the fund pays a special distribution at the end of the year. It has the highest expense ratio of 4.3% but has generated strong returns indicated by its 8.3% yield and best in class 19.0% 5-year price performance. Many analysts on Seeking Alpha tend to prefer CEFS over FOF for the ability to outperform over the long term.

High Income Securities Fund (PCF) is similar to RIV in that the distribution rate is higher at 11.4% but has a declining share price -18.9% and the highest turnover rate of 81%.

Over the past 10 years, as seen in the chart below, FOF generated 125.4% total return, falling second only to CEFS with 151.6%. In essence, an investment in FOF more than doubled its money for the investor.

Outlook

As the stock market has hit multiple highs recently, a prudent investor would be wise to look for defensive investments in my view. FOF provides both conservative active management and diversification which is a strong combination to prevent capital loss during a correction. FOF is well positioned during a stock market drop to continue paying the same monthly income with the ability to dollar cost average, achieving a lower cost basis.

Risks To Rating

Within the short term, many analysts are calling for a stock market correction as a result of the recent run up, which could adversely affect the share price of the CEF.

Another risk to the fund of fund strategies is that an investor may be able to outperform the fund by holding a few of the funds by themselves and not paying the management fee. What I like about FOF is that the funds are well selected, and if they are underperforming, C&S will switch them out. Therefore the fee for active management is worth it, in my view, to prevent holding the wrong funds.

Bottom Line

I rate Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund a Buy based on its consistent monthly distribution rate currently yielding 8%. It’s focus is on total return, so it not only pays a consistent monthly distribution but generates capital appreciation along the way. The fund holds a highly diverse set of closed end funds including bonds from PIMCO, commodities such as gold and silver, and assorted BlackRock equity allocation funds.

FOF is a consistent performer vs. peers. Run by Cohen & Steers, a highly reputable money manager, the fund has been in existence for 19 years and has the lowest expense ratio vs. competition. When benchmarked to typical indexes, FOF outperforms the real estate sector but falls short of the S&P 500. FOF has a strong total return of 125.4% over 10 years.

Risks to the fund include a drop in the stock market after achieving a record high, causing the NAV to trade at a discount. Investors could also just buy the funds individually, not paying the management fee for a fund of fund strategy. Within the sector, I regard Saba’s CEFs as the closest competitor, which may outperform FOF over the long term given their activist approach and payment of special distributions at the end of the year.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Volatile Friday Ends With Weekly Gain
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 experienced a volatile Friday, which ultimately ended with the index posting a minor weekly gain of 0.1%. This marks the fourth weekly gain in the past five weeks. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53.

This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new, then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%).

Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.96%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index that includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 14.75% year to date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 7.12% year to date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Gold: Triangle Formation Shows Indecision From Traders, Will $4,000 Hold? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Precious metals haven’t dodged the volatility bullet hitting markets throughout the morning session.

Bleak and brutal overnight trading has failed to attract flows into normally in-demand gold, even as risk sentiment deteriorates sharply. An unusual positive correlation between the yellow metal and equities is adding confusion about where capital rotates when these outflows occur.

After dropping $150 at its morning lows, mean-reverting buyers dragged gold prices back toward the $4,100 area and are attempting to break through the psychological level.

With fresh volatility, lower highs are forming, and the outlook is turning increasingly opaque. So let’s dive into a multi-timeframe gold analysis to see whether technical signals can help us determine where metal prices may be headed next.

Gold (XAU/USD) multi-timeframe technical analysis

Daily Chart

After forming a gigantic bearish divergence at the end of last month, brutal outflows brought gold well below the $4,000 mark. Still, some strong dip-buying pushed the metal back higher as late-trend followers rushed for the "discounted prices".

But discounts can be traps in markets. The sole performer during yesterday's bloody session, gold found a top in a flash sale this morning, all the way to $4,030.

Now back above $4,100, the price action just looks more confusing on the Daily chart. Long-wicked dojis like the one from today's action can put out trade setups:

  • Look at what happens if bulls manage to retake the daily highs at $4,211 (trend continuation)
  • Vice versa if bears bring the pair to new lows (especially below $4,000)

4H Chart and technical levels

Gold technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Current all-time High resistance: $4,250 to $4,400 (ATH $4,380)
  • Low of Resistance zone: $4,250 and Triangle formation top
  • Session highs: $4,211

Support Levels

  • 4H MA 200, Session and triangle formation lows: $4,030 to $4,050)
  • Major Pivot: $3,950 to $4,000
  • $3,700 consolidation Support
  • $3,500 Major Support

1H Chart

It's a bull and bear battle in today's action, as expressed in the consolidation patterns seen through higher timeframes. Buyers have broken the $4,100 level, but the momentum pivot stays around $4,110.

Closing above this level gives more odds for bull continuation towards the weekly close, while closing below the pivot gives back the hand to sellers.

Keep an eye on the triangle formation and watch the afternoon session closely to see if indecision follows or a side takes the wheel.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: WTI Crude Steadies Near $59 As Geopolitical Tensions Offset Oversupply Concerns
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

WTI crude oil attempted to stabilize on Friday, rising about 1 percent to trade near $59 after a multi-week slide that pushed the market into a heavy demand zone. The rebound marks the first meaningful pause in a two-week losing streak, helped by geopolitical risk and short-covering.

But the broader structure remains firmly bearish, with sellers continuing to defend every approach toward the descending resistance line that has capped the market since late June.

Bearish structure holds despite short-term bounce

On the daily chart, WTI remains anchored beneath its falling trendline that has guided the decline from $72. Price was rejected again this week near $61.90, consistent with every attempt to break through the descending ceiling. The moving averages underline that pressure.

WTI trades below the 20-day at $59.93, 50-day at $61.01, 100-day at $61.90, and 200-day at $62.45. This uniform alignment shows weakness across all timeframes and signals that any recovery remains corrective until price closes above at least the 100-day or 200-day EMA.

The Parabolic SAR has printed above price for more than two weeks, confirming that downward momentum remains in place even as intraday flows react to support.

The market recently retested the major level at $55.19, which acted as a pivot through the May and June consolidations. Friday’s bounce shows that buyers defended the zone, but the candles lacked strong lower-wick reversals, indicating that the move is driven more by position resets than by renewed confidence.

Supply outlook keeps pressure on the medium-term trend

While headlines out of Russia are supporting the latest rebound, the underlying fundamentals remain heavy. Ukrainian drone strikes on the Novorossiysk port and mounting stress across Russian trading operations have raised expectations of export disruptions.

Analysts warn that a third of Russia’s seaborne crude could face delays as sanctions tighten and rerouting becomes more difficult. Temporary pauses in Russian shipments to India and China add to the immediate supply risk.

However, the physical market continues to lean toward surplus. The IEA projects a 2.4 million barrels per day oversupply this year and 4 million next year, pointing to one of the most significant gluts in recent cycles.

OPEC’s Q3 data also reflected a surplus, while U.S. production continues to rise. U.S. inventories posted another build this week, reinforcing that the market is not tight enough to sustain durable bullish swings. As a result, the geopolitical premium has been overshadowed by structural excess.

WTI now trades between the $55 support zone and the descending trendline near $61. A breakout would require a close above $62.50, aligning with the 200-day EMA. Below, a failure to hold $55.19 would expose the next support near $52, opening the door to a deeper continuation of the downtrend.

In earlier analysis, we highlighted WTI’s inability to reclaim the descending trendline and warned that repeated failures near $62 would push the market back toward the $55 zone. This week’s price action aligns with that view, with the rebound developing only after sellers forced a retest of the demand shelf.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with around 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. data.title: Beyond Meat: Value Still Appears Limited In A Realistic Turnaround Scenario
data.description:
Beyond Meat, Inc. (BYND) temporarily achieved meme stock status and saw its stock briefly soar from around $0.50 to over $7 in under a week. However, the reality of Beyond Meat's financial challenges has brought its stock back down to earth.

Beyond Meat's business is struggling badly, although it does now have enough liquidity to likely minimize its bankruptcy risk until at least 2027. It issued approximately 59 million shares through its ATM equity program for around $149 million in net proceeds. It also reduced its convertible note debt by approximately $911 million through its exchange offer. The additional cash from the ATM equity program does improve its situation compared to my previous opinion on it.

I believe that Beyond Meat may end 2025 with approximately $220 million in cash on hand and $345 million in outstanding debt (most of which is due in 2030 now).

Beyond Meat's Q3 2025 earnings were fairly bleak, as its net revenues declined by 13.3% year-over-year (although this was in-line with its prior guidance). Beyond Meat's adjusted gross margin continued to be weak at 12.7% for the quarter.

It also provided guidance for a further deterioration in its net revenues for Q4 2025 and now appears likely to miss its original 2025 net revenue guidance by 16%. As well, Beyond Meat may end up with around 11% adjusted gross margin for 2025 compared to its initial guidance for 20% gross margins.

The intrinsic value of its stock would be zero if there is no business improvement from 2025 levels. A reasonable turnaround scenario (growing net revenues to $350 million in 2028 along with 28% gross margins) would still leave its shares with under $1 per share in intrinsic value.

Q3 2025 Results

Beyond Meat reported $70.2 million in net revenues for Q3 2025. This was close to the midpoint of its guidance for $68 million to $73 million in net revenues for the quarter, and also a decrease of 13.3% compared to Q3 2024.

Beyond Meat's adjusted gross margin was 12.7% in Q3 2025, excluding $1.7 million in expenses related to its exit from China. This was slightly lower than its 13.7% adjusted gross margin for Q2 2025.

Beyond Meat ended up with negative $21.6 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q3 2025, bringing its adjusted EBITDA loss up to $86 million for the first three quarters of the year.

Q4 Outlook And Full Year Performance

Beyond Meat now expects $60 million to $65 million in net revenues for Q4 2025. This would be a 15% to 22% decline compared to Q4 2024, which would be a worse performance than its -13.3% decline in Q3 2025.

Beyond Meat's initial 2025 guidance called for it to end up with $320 million to $335 million in net revenues. It is currently on track for approximately $276 million in 2025 net revenues (assuming it hits its Q4 2025 guidance midpoint). At $276 million, its net revenues would be 16% lower than the midpoint of its initial guidance.

I had also assumed (after its Q2 2025 earnings) that it would end up with around $280 million in 2025 net revenues, so it is trending marginally worse than that.

Beyond Meat initially expected 20% gross margins for 2025 and appears likely to end up with approximately 11% adjusted gross margins for the full year.

Beyond Meat reported $128 million in adjusted operating expenses in the first three quarters of 2025, including $40 million for Q3 2025. It thus seems to be on track to meet its original guidance for $160 million to $180 million in 2025 operating expenses.

Beyond Meat's Cash Position

Beyond Meat ended Q3 2025 with around $118 million in cash (including $1 million in restricted cash). It raised $149 million in net proceeds from its ATM equity program. After accounting for the exchange offer costs (from after the end of Q3 2025) and projected Q4 2025 cash burn, I am currently projecting Beyond Meat to end 2025 with $220 million in cash.

If Beyond Meat's business results stay at 2025 levels ($276 million in net revenues with 11% in adjusted gross margins), I believe it would end up with around $115 million per year in cash burn.

Beyond Meat also has $29.5 million in 2027 convertible notes that remain outstanding. Assuming those are paid back at maturity, Beyond Meat would run out of money in Q3 2027 (in the no business improvement scenario) without additional funding.

I've also mapped out a turnaround scenario that involves high-single digits net revenue growth in 2026 and 2027 and adjusted gross margins improving to 18% in 2026 and 23% in 2027. That scenario would leave Beyond Meat with approximately $30 million in cash at the end of 2027. It would run out of cash without additional funding in mid-to-late 2028 in that turnaround scenario.

Valuing Beyond Meat's Common Stock

The intrinsic value of Beyond Meat's common stock would be zero in a scenario with no business improvement from current levels. It would run out of cash in Q3 2027 without additional funding, and with PIK interest, it would also have around $380 million in projected outstanding debt at the end of 2027. Such a scenario would also have it generate around negative $100 million per year in adjusted EBITDA.

Beyond Meat's common stock may have some modest intrinsic value in what I'd consider a realistically achievable turnaround scenario. This scenario would see its net revenues grow by high-single digits per year, along with solid gross margin expansion.

This scenario would put it on track for $350 million in net revenues in 2028 (27% above 2025 levels) along with 28% gross margins in 2028. Beyond Meat has talked about getting back to 30+% gross margins, but has also fallen well short of gross margin expectations recently.

At the end of 2027 in this turnaround scenario, Beyond Meat would have $30 million in cash on hand and $380 million in outstanding debt, leaving it with $350 million in net debt.

Food companies such as Kraft Heinz and General Mills are valued at an enterprise value to sales multiple of approximately 2x, although they typically have better gross margins in the low-30s as well as double-digit adjusted EBITDA margins.

If we valued Beyond Meat at 2x 2028 net revenues in this turnaround scenario, that would give it an enterprise value of $700 million. With 453 million shares outstanding now, it would be worth around $0.77 per share. This would probably be generous, still, given that Beyond Meat's adjusted EBITDA margins are still likely to be negative in 2028.

Conclusion

Beyond Meat has significantly improved its liquidity and bought itself some more time with its debt exchange and its ATM equity offering. However, Beyond Meat's business results are still pretty dismal, with a -13.3% net revenue decline in Q3 2025 and an even larger net revenue decline expected in Q4 2025. Beyond Meat's gross margins have also fallen well short of expectations, and it is on track for an adjusted EBITDA loss of over $100 million for 2025.

If Beyond Meat can deliver high-single digits net revenue growth for the next few years, it may be able to get to $350 million in net revenues in 2028. I've also assumed that its adjusted gross margins can go from 11% in 2025 to 28% in 2028.

This would be a realistically achievable turnaround scenario for Beyond Meat, although one that would also be on the higher end of my expectations. Despite the business improvements, this scenario would still leave Beyond Meat in danger of running out of cash by late 2028 without additional funding. It would also still have negative adjusted EBITDA in 2028, and its shares would have an intrinsic value of under $1 per share based on comparable valuations.

data.title: Alcon: Some Premium Coming Out Of This Quality Play
data.description:
In August, I believed that there were more than meets the eye in case of Alcon Inc. (ALC). The undoubted leader in eye care enjoys a strong and diversified positioning, benefiting from tailwinds related to aging populations and technological advances.

The pending purchase STAAR Surgical (STAA) (and other smaller deals) were pressuring near term earnings, and despite solid sales growth and manageable debt, a near 30 times earnings multiple was more than fully valued, making me cautious.

Ever since, shares have come down from the $90 mark to the high-seventies, which resulted in meaningful valuation multiples compression. Shares still trade at a premium here, but a much more reasonable premiums, making shares look more compelling than they have been trading at in the (recent) past.

This means that some air has come out of the stock, which looks interesting, yet shares are not necessarily a screaming buy yet at current levels.

Eye Care

Alcon is a leading eye care business which is active in a wide range of unmet needs related to eye care. This includes cataract, retina, glaucoma, refractive, and dry eyes. These and other eye conditions are more prevalent, driven by aging populations, increased wealth, and a general higher prevalence of myopia.

The company has provided new eye care technologies for such conditions, in markets which generally are favorable with healthcare payment dynamics. In fact, nine out of ten visual impairments are preventable or curable according to Alcon, creating a >$35 billion global market, split between surgical and vision care. Products and solutions include equipment, consumables, lenses, and ocular health, with products sold across the globe.

Within this global market, Alcon is a giant, generating about $10 billion in sales, with nearly a billion spent every year on R&D efforts to fortify this leading position. Just like the market opportunity at large, Alcon´s sales are split pretty evenly between vision care and surgical, with the businesses active in ocular health, contact lenses, implantable, consumables, and equipment.

Originally part of Novartis (NVS), Alcon has been a standalone business from 2019 onwards, with the Swiss-American business doing well ever since.

Valuation Thoughts

Driven by a mix of organic and inorganic growth, Alcon has become this $10 billion giant, with 2024 sales reported up 5% to $9.8 billion. GAAP operating profits of $1.4 billion translate into earnings just over $2 per share. Adjusted earnings came in a dollar per share higher, driven by amortization charges, as a $3 earnings per share number looks realistic.

Trading around the $90 mark, this valued this well run business at 30 times earnings, as net debt of $3.6 billion translated into a <2 times leverage ratio with EBITDA reported around $2 billion per annum. The company guided for solid growth in 2025, with revenues seen up 6-8%, with adjusted earnings seen moving up in tandem to $3.20 per share.

M&A Gets Unleashed

In the spring, Alcon announced a $356 million strategic deal to acquire LENSAR (LNSR), this being a purchase for the advanced laser solution provider of cataracts. This was followed by another smaller deal for Aurion Biotech, an innovative cell therapy company, with both firms having a small near term impact (and likely incurring near term losses).

Following a 3% increase in first quarter sales, the company hiked the full-year sales guidance by $150 million to $10.45 billion, in between M&A impact and impact of a weaker dollar. However, the dilutive M&A efforts made that the adjusted earnings guidance was cut by ten cents to $3.10 per share.

In the meantime, the company announced a $1.5 billion deal to acquire Implantable Collamer Lens producer STAAR Surgical, a transaction set to boost net debt to $4.8 billion. Timed well, STAAR has been suffering from plunging sales in China, with its revenues being cut in half in the first half of this year.

Later, Alcon reported second quarter sales up 4%, yet despite this growth number, the company trimmed the full-year sales outlook by a hundred million to a midpoint $10.35 billion, while still guiding for adjusted earnings around $3.10 per share. Given these mixed trends, and a near 30 times multiple awarded to the shares, I was erring cautious, as shares have come down a bit since August.

Trends Cool Off

Since the downwards revisions to the full year sales outlook in August, shares of Alcon have come down a bit, having fallen to levels in the seventies, now trading at $78 per share.

In the meantime, the company closed on a bolt-on deal for LumiThera early in September, with no financial details announced on that purchase. This transaction is designed to treat early and intermediate dry age-related macular degeneration.

In November, Alcon reported a 6% increase in third quarter sales to $2.6 billion, although that constant currency growth came in at 5%. So far, the good news, as adjusted earnings of $0.79 per share were down two cents on the year before. Net debt was reported at $3.2 billion, no surprise as the STAAR purchase is still pending, with, in fact some of its STAAR's investors protesting against that deal.

In between all these trends, the company maintained the full-year sales and earnings guidance, all looking largely in line with expectations here. Trading at $78, the earnings multiple has come down from nearly 30 times at the start of the year, to about 25 times earnings, pushing up the earnings yield towards 4%.

This has substantially improved the appeal for equity holders, but a 25 times earnings multiple with about 4% organic growth is not necessarily a bargain here.

What Now?

In between the modest growth, but more compelling valuations here, the prospects for Alcon have improved here. Part of the valuation compression comes as the softer 2025 earnings growth is seen lasting into 2026 as well, as the company issued some cautionary comments here.

While the company cites tailwinds from new product launches, which are set to drive growth which will outpace that of the market, there are some headwinds as well. Alcon sees net incremental impact of tariffs to the 2026 results (over the 2025 results) by some $50-$100 million, which, among others, will have about a 40 basis point impact on margins. This could pressure pre-tax profits by about 8 cents per share in isolation.

This likely sets the company up for another year of non-impressive earnings growth, standing at the basis of the underperformance here. Right now, shares are back to levels first seen in 2021, and as most recent as early 2024, as valuation compression is evident, but shares still trade at a premium.

For now, there are quite some encouraging factors in between tariffs, currency moves, inflation, and uncertainty on the STAAR purchase. All these uncertainties and slower growth have been key reasons for the relative underperformance, and while appeal is on the increase, I am still non-convinced to get involved at these levels, looking for entry levels in the low seventies.

data.title: First Financial Bancorp Is A Stellar Prospect
data.description:
One very interesting player in the financial sector for investors to keep in mind is First Financial Bancorp. (FFBC). This is because of multiple factors, including a history of growth, high asset quality, the valuation of the stock, and, most interestingly, management's willingness to grow through acquisition. In fact, on Nov. 3, the company just completed a purchase. And in all likelihood, future purchases will be made. When you add all of these factors together, it's difficult not to like this kind of candidate. In fact, it is easy for me to rate it a very solid "Buy." And it wouldn't take much for me to upgrade it even more to a "Strong Buy."

Banking On First Financial Bancorp

Looking back at the most recent annual report provided by management, it's interesting to note that First Financial Bancorp is based out of Ohio, which is my home state. Admittedly, it is located in Cincinnati, which is around three hours from where I live. Even with that distance, however, I find it to be an interesting candidate solely because of its proximity to yours truly. This business is, according to management, a mid-sized, regional bank holding company. Through the locations that it has in operation, it engages in various banking activities, including commercial banking services as well as residential ones. Examples here include the provision of loans to individuals and businesses for the purchase of real estate, consumer financing activities, and more. It also engages in traditional consumer bank activities, like allowing customers to open bank accounts with it. On top of this, the company has its own wealth management division that offers up trust and wealth management services.

All combined, this is your typical regional bank. But what's really impressive about the business is that, over the last few difficult years for this sector, management has succeeded in growing the enterprise. From 2022 through 2024, deposits at the company jumped from $12.70 billion to $14.33 billion. And by the third quarter of 2025, deposits had increased even more to $14.43 billion. Even though I like seeing this kind of growth, one thing to keep in mind is that about 40.9% of its overall deposits are uninsured. I typically prefer this reading to be 30% or lower. And that's because, the higher it goes, the greater the risk profile if there is some sort of banking crisis.

As deposits have increased, the value of loans has also increased. They grew from $10.17 billion in 2022 to $11.61 billion last year. For the most recent quarter, however, they did contract slightly to $11.55 billion. About $4.05 billion, or 34.6%, of the entire loan portfolio of the business involves commercial real estate. But commercial and industrial loans are also very significant, accounting for $3.84 billion. Outside of that, its exposure is relatively limited, with residential real estate coming in at just $1.49 billion and home equity debt amounting to $936 million. All other lending categories, like credit cards, installment loans, construction real estate loans, and lease financing arrangements, are substantially smaller.

Even though the value of the loan portfolio for the institution has dropped lately, the value of securities has increased. After dropping from $3.64 billion in 2022 to $3.38 billion last year, First Financial Bancorp saw a rise to $3.61 billion in the third quarter of this year. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, have been on a constant decline since 2023, contracting from $1.01 billion to $739.7 million. But the good news is that debt has also been declining. And honestly, this is incredibly important to me. Back in 2022, this prospect had debt of $1.63 billion. But today, it stands at only $817 million. That's roughly a 50% drop in a little under four years. The fact that management was able to do this while seeing a general uptrend in loans is encouraging to me.

As the balance sheet for the company grew, so too did the income statement. The growth in the size of the balance sheet, as well as an increase in net interest margin from 3.77% to 4.05%, pushed net interest income up from $507.4 million to $564.4 million. And for this year so far, the $440.5 million achieved by the company is greater than the $419.4 million reported a year earlier. In the chart above, you can see that non-interest income and net profits have also gradually increased over time, with figures for this year looking particularly pleasant.

If we project out results based on what we have seen already this year, we can see in the chart below that shares of the company are trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of just 8.5. But you can see in that same chart something a little different as well. In addition to the company stacked up against five other similar firms, you can also see some pro forma adjustments that look at recent acquisition activities that the company has engaged in, both with synergies and without them.

In June of this year, management announced that it was acquiring Westfield Bancorp, in a deal valued at $325 million. 80% of the value of this transaction is in the form of cash, with the remainder in the form of stock. This purchase was ultimately completed on November 3rd. This purchase alone accelerated the firm's entry into northeast Ohio, which is where I live. It immediately adds about $2.2 billion in assets to First Financial Bancorp’s balance sheet. This includes $1.6 billion of loans. And that is fueled by the $1.9 billion in deposits that the institution has across these seven active branches that were operational at the time the deal was announced earlier this year.

If everything goes according to plan, this particular acquisition should increase First Financial Bancorp’s earnings per share by 11.8% next year. But that's not all. This is because, on Aug. 11 of this year, management also announced that they have decided to expand their presence into Chicago with the acquisition of BankFinancial Corporation (BFIN). This particular business has 18 retail locations in operation and $2.2 billion of overall deposits. This is a very intriguing purchase, costing shareholders only $142 million, based on the 0.48 of a share of First Financial Bancorp stock that shareholders of BankFinancial will receive for each share of BankFinancial that they currently own.

This is a rather large opportunity for investors. It gives the company a good foothold in the Chicago metropolitan statistical area, which is a market that boasts 9.2 million residents and total market deposits worth $596 billion. The 3.5 million households in the area are responsible for $860 billion of annual GDP. And with a median household income of $86,600, this is a market that is solidly middle class. This deal is being done at a price-to-tangible book value multiple of only 0.91 and will result in shareholders of First Financial Bancorp giving away only 6% of the combined business. The goal here is to capture significant cost savings amounting to 45% of BankFinancial’s annual operating expenses, with 75% of these savings captured next year. There will be some significant restructuring here, including the sale of the entire $500 million multifamily loan portfolio that BankFinancial currently has on its books.

In the aforementioned chart, I also valued First Financial Bancorp based on pro forma figures for these purchases, both with synergies and without them. For context, we are looking at cost savings from these two purchases of around $33.9 million. This is on top of the $25.8 million in net profits that the two acquisitions should bring to the table. In the chart below, I then compared First Financial Bancorp to the same five companies I compared it to already, using both the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. We can see that these purchases do make First Financial Bancorp a bit more expensive on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis compared to what it would be otherwise. But even so, the company is trading at a discount to book value. It's only on a price-to-tangible-book basis that shares are a bit lofty.

In addition to all of this, I would argue that First Financial Bancorp looks very attractive when it comes to asset quality. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets without factoring in these acquisitions. The reading of 1.54% is well above the 1% minimum that I like to see. It also puts the company quite a bit above any of the five institutions that I compared it to already. Likewise, the 11.08% return on equity shown in the subsequent chart puts it above the 10% minimum I like to see. And, as was the case with return on assets, it makes the company superior to these other five institutions.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I firmly believe that First Financial Bancorp is an attractive prospect. There is some uncertainty regarding these acquisitions, especially whether management can capture the promised synergies or not. But even if they can't, I don't consider these purchases to be all that bad. And if they can, I would make the claim that the stock is certainly very attractive. This is true on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms. And when you add on top of this, it's already high asset quality, and the fact that the business has done well to grow in recent years leading up to this point, I have no reason not to rate it a very solid "Buy."

data.title: Interfor: An Increasingly Problematic Capital Structure
data.description:
Company Overview

With a portfolio of 28 manufacturing facilities across North America, Interfor (IFP:CA) is the third largest lumber producer on the continent, only behind West Fraser Timber (WFG), and Weyerhaeuser (WY), two companies I have recently covered. To be more precise, Interfor produced 4.0 billion board feet of lumber in 2024 compared to 4.4 and 5.3 billion board feet produced by Weyerhaeuser and West Fraser, respectively.

After including other important players like Canfor, Sierra Pacific, and Georgia-Pacific, the lumber industry rapidly becomes fragmented, with a long tail of relatively small producers. Given the ongoing industry downturn, this could lead to additional consolidation, especially knowing that West Fraser and Weyerhaeuser sit on strong liquidity positions. Unfortunately, the picture is quite different for Interfor, as its capital structure looks increasingly vulnerable amidst current market conditions and tariff uncertainties. This is a key topic I will discuss in more detail later in this article.

To be clear, I do not view size in itself as a competitive advantage for a lumber producer, although it can indirectly lead to better access to capital and enhanced diversification at the corporate level. At the end of the day, every single sawmill is affected by unique supply and demand fundamentals, as transporting logs over too long distances can rapidly render a manufacturing facility uncompetitive. What is the cost of bringing the raw wood fibre, the raw logs, into the yard? This is where the real competitive advantages are for a sawmill.

Ironically, restricted log supply is one of the most important problems facing the forest industry in British Columbia. This is in part due to historic infestations of mountain pine beetle that decimated harvestable volumes in some parts of the province. For Interfor, the mitigating factors are the locations of its sawmills within British Columbia and the fact that the province accounted for a mere ~15% of total production volumes in 2024.

A Pure-Play Lumber Producer

What puts Interfor apart from its two largest publicly listed peers is its core focus on lumber production. As illustrated in the chart below, lumber represented ~80% of IFP's total sales in 2024, with engineered wood products like I-joist having a minimal contribution of about 5%.

In contrast, West Fraser is well-known as the largest producer of oriented strand board in North America, with lumber sales representing ~40% of total revenues. In Weyerhaeuser's case, lumber sales move the needle even less, as they represented ~25% of total revenues in 2024. As a reminder, WY's lumber revenues are only a part of the sizable Wood Products segment, which is in turn diversified away by the world's largest portfolio of timberlands.

In short, Interfor would be the way to go for investors looking for a pure-play exposure to the North American lumber market. However, this comes with the challenges associated with a vulnerable capital structure in the midst of a severe industry downcycle. In a sense, the timing of the next industry upcycle is becoming increasingly important for Interfor's shareholders, as if it takes too long, they may be forced into a reorganization with creditors before the recovery occurs, at least in my view. If I want to capitalize on the current industry downturn, I need to make sure that the entities I am exposed to will still be alive when the upcycle shows up. It is from this perspective that I view Interfor's common stock as a call option on the North American lumber market, and like any other option, it also comes with an expiration date. Can a recovery happen fast enough?

As illustrated below, IFP has a strong presence in pretty much every key forestry region on the continent, with the southern United States accounting for approximately 50% of Interfor's annual production capacity of 4.7 billion board feet. In terms of actual production volume, the United States accounts for ~60% of the total, with only 25% of production being exported from Canada to the United States and subject to tariffs or duties. The comprehensive geographical diversification is certainly interesting, but it does not solve the core balance sheet issues.

A Vulnerable Capital Structure

As illustrated in the capitalization table below, Interfor ended September 2025 with net debt of CAD$755 million. With a book equity of CAD$1.4 billion, the leverage in the capital structure looks reasonable at only 35% of total invested capital. However, if we replace the equity book value with its current market value of ~$500 million, the picture becomes quite different, as the leverage ratio increases to 60%. Which number is the most reflective of today's market environment? An accounting number or a market number? After all, as we have seen with the CAD$144 million bought deal offering that closed on October 1st, Intefor is only able to raise capital based on market prices, not accounting values. Of note, the table below reflects the impact of the recent capital raise.

In terms of capital allocation, it is interesting to note that Interfor launched a substantial issuer bid to purchase CAD$100 million worth of common stock by way of a modified Dutch auction. In mid-September 2022, the company purchased 3.4 million common shares at an average price of $29.80.

In this context, how should we interpret the fact that IFP just sold to the market 14.3 million shares at an average price of CAD$10.05? Is it not the very definition of buying high and selling low? In cyclical and capital-intensive industries like the one Interfor is operating in, deploying capital in a counter-cyclical manner is absolutely key. Unfortunately, management has done the exact opposite with the share repurchases of September 2022, followed by the capital raise of October 2025 executed at a share price ~65% lower.

With available liquidity slightly below CAD$250 million at the end of September 2025, Interfor is also not in the best position to capitalize on the current industry downturn. In contrast, West Fraser ended the third quarter with a net cash balance of $212 million, while Weyerhaeuser and PotlatchDeltic are supported by the predictable and resilient timberland portfolios. When thinking about the overall North American forest industry, I view Interfor a lot more like a target than a suitor. In a distressed situation, could Interfor's well-diversified portfolio attract the interest of its larger and better-funded peers? If downturns are generally synonymous with industry consolidation, I would answer with the affirmative.

High Capital Intensity

In the first nine months of 2025, Interfor generated operating cash flows of CAD$45.4 million and deployed CAD$66.9 million in capital expenditures, meaning that the CAD$21.5 million shortfall had to be funded from the balance sheet. The situation in Q3-25 was even worse, as operating cash flows were negative by CAD$26.3 million, and the capital consumed by the operations was on top of the CAD$28.9 million in capital expenditure. Once combined, we are talking about a liquidity drain of CAD$55.2 million in a single quarter. In this context, the ~CAD$250 million of available liquidity at the end of September 2025 looks quite small.

Capital expenditures are expected at around CAD$75 million in 2026, a ~20% reduction compared to the CAD$92.5 million expected for the full year of 2025. Based on the chart below, this is probably the minimum capital expenditures to maintain the asset base in good standing. Assuming that lumber prices remain depressed for another 18 to 24 months, where will Interfor find the required capital if the operations themselves are consuming capital?

Lumber Prices

In the third quarter of 2025, Interfor's average realized lumber price was CAD$618 per million board feet, a meaningful decline from the CAD$712 and CAD$684 recorded in the first and second quarters of 2025, respectively. As illustrated below, the most pronounced changes were in Southern Yellow Pine prices, a species of pine representing the bulk of the production volumes coming out of the southern United States. For Interfor, the Southern Yellow Pine is also the most important tree species, having accounted for 46% of the company's total production volumes in the first nine months of 2025.

When looking at capital flows within the North American forest industry, the capital is going out of British Columbia and flows into the southern United States. This is also where the levels of investment are the strongest relative to the other North American basins, meaning that it is also where we can find some volume growth. With sluggish lumber demand and resilient production volumes in the U.S. South, should we be surprised that Southern Yellow Pine prices were hit the hardest?

Talking about production volumes, I think it is worth highlighting Interfor's press release of early September 2025 announcing production cuts totalling 145 million board feet between September and December 2025, representing roughly 12% of the company's total production. Just a few weeks later, in mid-October 2025, Interfor announced that production cuts for the fourth quarter were now expected at 250 million board feet or about 26% of a normal operating stance. The company also mentioned that the production cuts were evenly split between the United States and Canada, with all operating regions being impacted.

Large production cuts like the one announced by Interfor are a step in the right direction and reflect how challenging the market environment truly is right now. To cut production levels to such a degree necessarily means that multiple sawmills are not even able to cover variable operating costs. How long will the downturn last?

As discussed in my recent article about Boise Cascade (BCC), I think it has a lot to do with 30-year mortgage rates and ultimately with long-term Treasury yields. Given my views on long-term interest rates in the United States, I tend to think the current downturn may last longer than most people expect and could even get worse from here. Even if the Fed reduces overnight interest rates, the impact on 30-year mortgage rates has been pretty muted, at least so far. Knowing that central banks only control the short end of the interest rate curve, what is the next catalyst for the U.S. housing market? This is a question I have trouble finding an answer to.

Given the deteriorating geopolitical landscape all around the world, including in the Middle East or around Taiwan, could the next major crisis be a war? If it is the case, what happens to 10-year Treasury yields and 30-year mortgage rates in the United States?

Conclusion

With an annual production capacity of 4.7 billion board feet, Interfor is the third largest lumber producer in North America. The company differentiates itself from West Fraser and Weyerhaeuser by focusing on lumber production, which accounts for roughly 80% of annual revenues. IFP's portfolio is well-diversified geographically, with a large presence in every North American basin.

My 'Sell' rating primarily reflects my concerns about the strength of the capital structure if weak lumber prices were to persist for a few more quarters. As a reminder, Interfor burned cash at the operating level in Q3-25, and this is before taking into account the capital expenditures required to maintain the asset base not only in good standing, but also competitive in the global North American marketplace.

Without any material improvement in lumber prices in general and in southern yellow pine prices in particular, I would expect IFP's share price to continue to drift lower over time, similar to an out-of-the-money call option getting closer and closer to maturity. The clock is ticking for Interfor's shareholders, as the next upcycle must arrive before the company's liquidity position gets too tight. With capital expenditures of CAD$75 million planned for 2026, the pressure on the capital structure would increase by another notch if operating cash flows were to remain depressed for another year.

Given that the North American lumber industry is still fragmented, Interfor's portfolio could become an interesting asset for larger peers like West Fraser and Weyerhaeuser. First, IFP's portfolio has the scale to move the needle even in the largest portfolio, and second, the purchase price could become too good to ignore if market conditions worsen even further.
